summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s ideals—modularity, open source, and avoiding lock-in—are less important to most buyers than consolidated, tightly integrated suites that simply work. Microsoft’s dominance in BI and databases shows that customers often prefer convenience, bundling, and sales reach over craft and composability. The likely future, he suggests, is consolidation, not a sprawling best‑of‑breed ecosystem.
money_quotes: Microsoft is a dominant player in the data industry.
This, combined with Microsoft’s dominant position in the market, suggests that customers actually don’t care about modularity.
We don’t want to be locked in, but more than that, we don’t want to have to migrate at all.

Most people “don’t care about the aesthetics of software in the same way non-plumbers don’t care about the aesthetics of plumbing—they just want their information in the right place or their hot water to work.”
Consolidation, it turns out, may be less of economic necessity, and more of a product opportunity.
key_insight: For most data buyers, the practical benefits of a consolidated, integrated suite outweigh the ideals of modularity and openness, making consolidation the more realistic winning strategy.
